name: later
description: Unicode Collation Algorithm implementation in Zig
readme:
# later – Unicode collation in Zig

This is a Zig implementation of the
[Unicode Collation Algorithm](https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr10/), using no
dependencies beyond the standard library. `later` passes the conformance tests
for both the Default Unicode Collation Element Table (DUCET) and the root
collation order of the Common Locale Data Repository (CLDR). You can verify this
by running the tests:

```sh
zig build test --summary line --release=safe
```

## Usage example

```zig
const std = @import("std");
const later = @import("later");

test "sort multilingual list of names" {
    const alloc = std.testing.allocator;

    var coll = try later.Collator.initDefault(alloc);
    defer coll.deinit();

    var input = [_][]const u8{
        "چنگیز",
        "Éloi",
        "Ötzi",
        "Melissa",
        "صدام",
        "Mélissa",
        "Overton",
        "Elrond",
    };

    const expected = [_][]const u8{
        "Éloi",
        "Elrond",
        "Melissa",
        "Mélissa",
        "Ötzi",
        "Overton",
        "چنگیز",
        "صدام",
    };

    std.mem.sortUnstable([]const u8, &input, &coll, later.collateComparatorOrPanic);
    try std.testing.expectEqualSlices([]const u8, &expected, &input);
}
```

## Further notes

`later` is based on my somewhat more mature UCA implementation in Rust,
[feruca](https://github.com/theodore-s-beers/feruca). I wanted to learn Zig
properly, and Unicode collation is a good, non-trivial problem to solve toward
that end. Perhaps this collator will also be of use to others in the Zig
community.

It's worth noting that a substantial part of the work of implementing the UCA is
generating maps from Unicode data files. We need to be able to determine
quickly, for a given code point, its canonical decomposition, combining class,
collation weights, and more. I built the maps for `later` in a separate
repository, [uca-maps-zig](https://github.com/theodore-s-beers/uca-maps-zig).
They're serialized in simple custom binary formats (see `src/bin`) and loaded
on-demand in collation.

Another part of the fun in this project was implementing UTF-8
decoding/validation and Unicode form NFD normalization. It turns out the
algorithms are well established, and they work nicely, and they aren't all that
difficult to code. We stand on the shoulders of giants!

releases.name: v0.3.0
releases.body:
## Notes on this minor (breaking) release

- I decided, given the changes in Zig 0.16 and the new constraints imposed via `std.Io`, that it would be better to simplify how this library works.
- No implementation of `std.Io` is needed here anymore, not even a fake one like I was using in the last patch release. I got rid of the mutex on `Collator` and changed it so that it just eagerly loads all the data that it might need on initialization. The perf impact of this change is small enough that it's a clear win given the reduced complexity of the code.
- The `getX` helper methods, which look up values in the maps of Unicode data, become one-liners and no longer need to be treated as fallible. This allowed removing some `try` calls throughout the library, which in turn allowed for the return types of a few higher-level functions to be simplified.
- The behavior of `Collator`'s `init` method should also now be safer; it will clean up after itself properly if it fails on any of the `try` steps (i.e., steps that allocate).
- I had realized over time that the public API of this library was in a potentially misleading shape.
   - `Collator` should have one clear default method, `collate`, which should be _fallible_. It now does.
   - If we want a version of `collate` that has a simple return type and panics in the astronomically unlikely event of allocation failure, then it should be named clearly. That is now called `collateOrPanic`.
   - The only comparator function that it makes sense to offer users is on the "or panic" path. This is because a comparator needs to have a simple `bool` return type in order to be plugged into sort functions. So now there is just one comparator offered as a convenience, called `collateComparatorOrPanic`.
- There was a subtle bug in my UTF-8 decoding logic, relating to the handling of invalid byte sequences. I fixed that and added a bunch of test cases, several of them taken directly from examples in the Unicode standard. (See, in [version 17](https://www.unicode.org/versions/Unicode17.0.0/UnicodeStandard-17.0.pdf) of the standard, section 3.9.5 "Constraints on Conversion Processes," and section 3.9.6 "`U+FFFD` Substitution of Maximal Subparts.")
- I also fixed an edge case in the `collate` method, in the early return path for the case where one string is found to be a prefix of the other after NFD normalization. Prefix-trimming can actually reveal that two strings are _equivalent_ after normalization: they would both have a length of `0` after the shared prefix. In that case, we can immediately return an `Order` of `.eq`, but only if the `tiebreak` option is `false`. I added a bit of extra logic to skip the early return if the result would be `.eq` but `tiebreak` is `true`.

## PRs since the last release

- fix utf-8 decoder, simplify api by @theodore-s-beers in https://github.com/theodore-s-beers/later/pull/5
- simplify architecture further by @theodore-s-beers in https://github.com/theodore-s-beers/later/pull/6
- simplify function return type by @theodore-s-beers in https://github.com/theodore-s-beers/later/pull/7
- increment minor version by @theodore-s-beers in https://github.com/theodore-s-beers/later/pull/8


**Full changelog:** https://github.com/theodore-s-beers/later/compare/v0.2.3...v0.3.0

releases.name: v0.2.3
releases.body:
In this patch release:

- Updated the library to conform to Zig 0.16. The new `std.Io` system required some changes under the hood, but fortunately the API can stay the same.

Full changelog: https://github.com/theodore-s-beers/later/compare/v0.2.2...v0.2.3

releases.name: v0.2.2
releases.body:
In this patch release:

- Updated the library to conform to Zig 0.15. There were breaking changes to the std lib, for example with `ArrayList`.
- Did some refactoring, mostly to reduce redundancy, though perhaps with a slight performance benefit (a couple of "cold paths" were removed).
- Simplified processing of sort keys, in particular at the quaternary level, which is better viewed as a modified re-run of the primary level.

Full changelog: https://github.com/theodore-s-beers/later/compare/v0.2.1...v0.2.2

releases.name: v0.2.1
releases.body:
In this patch release:

- I was persuaded to switch back to an approach whereby all allocation fallibility is "handled" throughout the library's code, and it can then be disregarded (i.e., caught to panic) at the top level. Collation logic lives under a function called `collateFallible`, which is then wrapped by `collate`. Users who prefer to allow for allocation failure can do so—though it has some frustrating implications. I understand that this is fundamental to the design of the Zig language.

Full changelog: https://github.com/theodore-s-beers/later/compare/v0.2.0...v0.2.1

releases.name: v0.2.0
releases.body:
In this minor version release:

- The `Collator` API is a bit simpler, since `init` no longer requires `try`. Rather, if the modest initial allocations fail, it will panic.
- The "panic on allocation failure" approach is now followed throughout the library, simplifying many code paths. `Collator` makes as few allocations as possible but needs a certain amount of memory—between 1.7 and 1.8 MiB total, when running the UCA conformance tests—and if that isn't available, there isn't much that we can do.

Full changelog: https://github.com/theodore-s-beers/later/compare/v0.1.1...v0.2.0

releases.name: v0.1.1
releases.body:
In this patch release:

- We switch to a faster method of looking up a code point's canonical combining class.
- The conformance test function is made a bit more efficient.
- Various little details in the code are cleaned up.

Full changelog: https://github.com/theodore-s-beers/later/compare/v0.1.0...v0.1.1

releases.name: v0.1.0
releases.body: This is an initial release of the library, to facilitate adding it as a dependency.

owner_bio: Research software engineer, Persian and Arabic philologist
